title: (GL_k × S_n)-Modules of Multivariate Diagonal Harmonics
overall_summary: The paper studies the stable spaces E_n of multivariate diagonal harmonic polynomials as simultaneous GL_k and S_n modules, encoding their multigraded Hilbert series by GL characters and their S_n structure by Frobenius characteristics. Its central structural framework is the Schur-⊗-Schur expansion E_n = Σ_{λ,μ} c_{λ,μ} s_λ ⊗ s_μ, whose finite-k restrictions are obtained by evaluating the GL-side Schur functions in k variables. For hook-shaped Schur coefficients, the most directly useful proven tool is the plethystic hook-extraction identity f[q−εu]/(q+u) = Σ_{a,b} ⟨f,s_(a|b)⟩ q^a u^b, together with Proposition 1, which gives a product formula for the hook part of the alternating component A_n = ⟨E_n,e_n(z)⟩. Several broader statements that would give strong hook coefficient information are explicitly conjectural in the paper, notably the Hook-Components conjecture, the Coefficient-Length conjecture, the Delta-by-Skewing conjecture, and a hook-hook multiplicity formula. The paper is therefore most useful as a source of GL-character formalism, plethystic hook extraction, product/q-binomial hook identities, and conjectural structural reductions linking hook data to the alternating component and Delta-operator combinatorics.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Schur-⊗-Schur character decomposition, Section 2, Equations (2.1)-(2.3)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R_n = Q[x_{ij} : i ≥ 1, 1 ≤ j ≤ n] be the polynomial ring in infinitely many rows of n variables, with row-degree grading. The symmetric group S_n acts by permuting columns, and GL_k acts on the first k rows by left multiplication; these actions commute. If M is a homogeneous polynomial GL_k × S_n submodule of R_n, then its multigraded GL character together with its S_n Frobenius characteristic has an expansion M(q;z) = Σ_{μ ⊢ n} Σ_λ a_{λ,μ} s_λ(q) s_μ(z), with nonnegative integer multiplicities a_{λ,μ}. Equivalently, in the paper's Schur-⊗-Schur notation, M = Σ_{μ ⊢ n} Σ_λ a_{λ,μ} s_λ ⊗ s_μ, where the left Schur function records the GL character and the right Schur function records the S_n irreducible. For the stable diagonal harmonic module E_n, the coefficient of s_μ(z) is denoted c_μ = Σ_λ c_{λ,μ} s_λ, and the finite-k character is obtained by evaluating the GL-side Schur functions at q_1,...,q_k, equivalently setting q_i = 0 for i > k.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The row-degree grading is exactly the weight decomposition for the diagonal torus of GL_k: a monomial with row-degree d = (d_1,d_2,...) contributes weight q^d. Since M is a polynomial GL_k representation over characteristic zero and the S_n action commutes with GL_k, M decomposes into simultaneous irreducibles W_λ ⊗ V_μ, where W_λ is the irreducible polynomial GL_k representation of highest weight λ and V_μ is the irreducible S_n representation indexed by μ. The character of W_λ is s_λ(q), and the Frobenius characteristic of V_μ is s_μ(z). Summing these characters over multiplicities gives the displayed expansion. Restriction from GL_∞ to GL_k is realized on characters by setting all q_i with i > k equal to zero.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Stability and support of E_n, Section 2.1, quoted from Bergeron [7]
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For each k, E_n^{<k>} is defined as the smallest graded S_n submodule of Q[x_{ij} : 1 ≤ i ≤ k, 1 ≤ j ≤ n] that contains the Vandermonde determinant V_n in the first row and is closed under all partial derivatives and all higher polarization operators Σ_{r=1}^n u_r ∂_{v_r}^j, where u and v are rows of variables and j ≥ 1. These modules form a filtration Q = E_n^{<0>} ⊂ E_n^{<1>} ⊂ E_n^{<2>} ⊂ ... whose stable limit is E_n. The paper states, citing [7], that stability is reached at k = n−1. Consequently, in the Schur-⊗-Schur expansion of E_n, no GL-side partition λ with more than n−1 parts occurs, and no λ of size greater than binomial(n,2) occurs.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The closure under polarization operators gives E_n^{<k>} the structure of a GL_k submodule, while the column-permutation action gives the commuting S_n action. The paper does not reprove the stability theorem, but cites [7] for the fact that adding rows beyond n−1 introduces no new stable GL irreducibles. The bound |λ| ≤ binomial(n,2) is compatible with total degree: V_n has total degree binomial(n,2), partial derivatives lower degree, and the polarization operators used do not increase total degree.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Classical one- and two-row specializations, Section 2.1, Equation (2.4)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The paper records two standard specializations of the stable character E_n. With one GL parameter q, E_n(q;z) = h_n[z/(1−q)] / h_n[1/(1−q)], in plethystic notation. With two GL parameters q,t, E_n(q,t;z) = ∇(e_n)(q,t;z), where ∇ is the Macdonald eigenoperator and e_n is the elementary symmetric function of degree n in the Frobenius variables z.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: These identifications are quoted as known. The one-row case is the classical graded S_n-harmonic or coinvariant module, whose Frobenius characteristic is the one-row modified Macdonald specialization h_n[z/(1−q)]/h_n[1/(1−q)]. The two-row case is the Garsia-Haiman diagonal harmonic module, whose bigraded Frobenius characteristic is ∇(e_n). The paper uses these formulas as inputs and examples for the stable GL_∞ framework.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Ungraded specialization at k variable sets, Section 2.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a GL-side Schur function s_λ, evaluation at k ones is s_λ(k) = s_λ(1,1,...,1,0,...) = Π_{(i,j)∈λ} (k + j − i)/h(i,j), where h(i,j) is the hook length of the cell (i,j). Therefore E_n(k;z), the ungraded Frobenius characteristic of E_n^{<k>}, is polynomial in k. The paper gives the following special cases: E_n(0;z) = s_n(z); E_n(1;z) = Σ_{μ ⊢ n} f^μ s_μ(z) = e_1(z)^n, where f^μ is the number of standard Young tableaux of shape μ; and E_n(2;z) = Σ_{μ ⊢ n} (−1)^{n−ℓ(μ)} (n+1)^{ℓ(μ)−1} z_μ^{-1} p_μ(z) = (1/(n+1)) e_n[(n+1)z]. Consequently dim E_n^{<2>} = (n+1)^{n−1} and dim A_n^{<2>} = (1/(n+1)) binomial(2n,n), where A_n denotes the alternating component.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The evaluation formula for s_λ(k) is the standard hook-content formula for polynomial GL_k characters. The case k = 0 is immediate from E_n^{<0>} = Q with trivial S_n type s_n. The case k = 1 is the classical coinvariant algebra/harmonic space, a graded version of the regular representation, giving Frobenius characteristic e_1^n after forgetting the grading. The case k = 2 is the parking-function Frobenius characteristic, quoted from Stanley's formula; the dimension statements follow by pairing with p_1^n for the total dimension and with e_n for the alternating multiplicity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Macdonald hook coefficient identity, Appendix, Equations (6.7)-(6.8)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H̃_μ(q,t;z) be the combinatorial modified Macdonald polynomial indexed by μ, and let B_μ = Σ_{(i,j)∈μ} q^i t^j, using the paper's cell-coordinate convention. For a hook partition (a|b) = (a+1,1^b) of n, the paper records the identity ⟨H̃_μ, s_(a|b)⟩ = e_b[B_μ − 1]. In particular, ⟨H̃_μ, s_{1^n}⟩ = T_μ, where T_μ = Π_{(i,j)∈μ} q^i t^j, and for the one-row partition n one has ⟨H̃_n, s_(a|b)⟩ = q^{binomial(b+1,2)} [n−1 choose b]_q, where [n−1 choose b]_q is the Gaussian q-binomial coefficient.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites these as standard Macdonald-polynomial identities, referring to formulas equivalent to exercises in Macdonald's book. For the one-row shape μ = n, the alphabet B_n − 1 is q + q^2 + ... + q^{n−1}. Therefore e_b[B_n − 1] is the elementary symmetric function of degree b in q,q^2,...,q^{n−1}, which equals q^{1+2+...+b} [n−1 choose b]_q = q^{binomial(b+1,2)} [n−1 choose b]_q.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Plethystic hook-coefficient extraction, Section 4.1, Equations (4.1)-(4.2)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Use Frobenius notation (a|b) for the hook partition (a+1,1^b). Let ε be the plethystic symbol satisfying p_r[ε] = (−1)^r. The paper states that s_(a|b)[q−εu]/(q+u) = q^a u^b, and that s_ν[q−εu] = 0 whenever ν is not a hook. Hence, for any symmetric function f with Schur expansion, f[q−εu]/(q+u) = Σ_{a,b ≥ 0} ⟨f,s_(a|b)⟩ q^a u^b. Thus the coefficients of q^a u^b in this two-variable plethystic specialization are exactly the hook-Schur multiplicities of f.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper uses the plethystic skewing identity f[q−εu] = Σ_{r ≥ 0} u^r (e_r^⊥ f)(q), together with the dual Pieri rule. For a hook s_(a|b), the only vertical-strip removals that leave a one-row shape, and hence survive evaluation in the single variable q, are the removal of the b cells in the leg, leaving a row of size a+1, and the removal of the b+1 cells in the first column, leaving a row of size a. These two contributions give q^{a+1}u^b + q^a u^{b+1} = (q+u)q^a u^b. Non-hook Schur functions vanish under the same virtual alphabet specialization q−εu. Expanding an arbitrary f in the Schur basis and using linearity gives the stated hook-generating identity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1, hook restriction property for the alternating component, Section 4.1, Equation (4.3)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A_n = ⟨E_n,e_n(z)⟩ be the GL_∞ character of the alternating S_n-isotypic component of E_n, where e_n(z) = s_{1^n}(z). Then A_n[q−εu] = (q+u)(q^2+u)···(q^{n−1}+u). Combined with the hook-extraction identity, this says that the multiplicity of the GL-side hook Schur function s_(r|s) in A_n is the coefficient of q^r u^s in (q^2+u)(q^3+u)···(q^{n−1}+u). Equivalently, by expanding the product, this multiplicity counts subsets T of {2,3,...,n−1} such that |T| is the number of u-factors chosen and the sum of the complementary q-exponents is r.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof recalls the Macdonald hook coefficient formula for the one-row specialization: for a+b = n−1, ⟨E_n(q;z), s_(a|b)(z)⟩ = q^{binomial(b+1,2)} [n−1 choose b]_q. The paper then uses the relation between these one-variable hook coefficients and the skewings e_a^⊥ A_n, writing (e_a^⊥ A_n)(q) = ⟨E_n(q;z), s_(a|b)(z)⟩. Applying the plethystic identity A_n[q−εu] = Σ_{a ≥ 0} u^a (e_a^⊥ A_n)(q) gives A_n[q−εu] = Σ_{a+b=n−1} q^{binomial(b+1,2)} [n−1 choose b]_q u^a. The q-binomial theorem rewrites this last sum as Π_{i=1}^{n−1}(q^i+u). Dividing by q+u and using the hook-extraction identity gives the stated hook multiplicity interpretation for A_n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Length components and extremal components, Section 4.3, Equations (4.5)-(4.9)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Write E_n = Σ_{μ ⊢ n} c_μ ⊗ s_μ, with c_μ = Σ_λ c_{λ,μ} s_λ. For d ≥ 0, define the length-d component E_n^{(d)} = Σ_{μ ⊢ n} c_μ^{(d)} ⊗ s_μ, where c_μ^{(d)} = Σ_{ℓ(λ)=d} c_{λ,μ} s_λ. The paper defines the degree of a Schur-positive expression as the maximum |λ| appearing on the GL side and states that deg(c_μ^{(j)}) = binomial(n,2) − binomial(j,2) − Σ_{i∈μ} binomial(i,2). It also gives the extremal values E_n^{(0)} = 1 ⊗ s_n and E_n^{(n−1)} = e_{n−1} ⊗ e_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper presents these as structural properties of the length decomposition and illustrates them with explicit examples, but does not include a full proof in the supplied text. The definitions separate the stable GL character by the number of parts of the GL partition λ. The extremal component E_n^{(0)} corresponds to the GL-constant part with trivial S_n type, while E_n^{(n−1)} is the maximal-length alternating contribution. The stated degree formula records the maximum total GL degree possible in each length and S_n-isotypic component.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Conditional Lemma 5.1, Delta-by-Skewing implies the coefficient-length prediction for μ_1 = 2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume the paper's Delta-by-Skewing conjecture, which in displayed form states that for n = a+b+1, ∆'_{e_a} e_n(z) = ((e_b^⊥ ⊗ Id) E_n)(q,t;z). Then the paper derives Lemma 5.1: the Coefficient-Length conjecture ℓ(⟨E_n,s_μ⟩) = n−μ_1 holds for every partition μ of n with first part μ_1 = 2. More explicitly, for μ = (2^k,1^{n−2k}), the same discussion gives ⟨e_{n−2}^⊥ E_n, s_(2^k,1^{n−2k})⟩ = Σ_{i=k−1}^{n−k−1} s_i and consequently ⟨E_n, s_(2^k,1^{n−2k})⟩ has top-length contribution Σ_{i=k−1}^{n−k−1} s_{i+1,1^{n−3}} plus terms of length less than n−2, as in Equation (5.11).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The argument uses the known identity, cited from Haglund-Remmel-Wilson, ∆_{e_1}(e_n) = Σ_{k=1}^n s_{k−1} ⊗ e_{n−k} e_k, together with ∆'_{e_1}(e_n) = ∆_{e_1}(e_n) − 1 ⊗ e_n. The Schur expansion in the z-variables of e_{n−k}e_k only involves partitions with first part at most 2. Under Delta-by-Skewing, this support information translates into vanishing and nonvanishing statements for elementary skewings e_r^⊥ of the GL coefficients ⟨E_n,s_μ⟩. For μ with μ_1 = 2, the nonzero skewed expression displayed above detects the predicted maximal length n−2 and determines the leading length contribution. The paper states this as a conditional implication from Conjecture 1.

proof_techniques.name: Encode multigrading as a GL character
proof_techniques.description: The paper treats the m sets of variables as rows of a matrix. The diagonal torus of GL_m records the multidegree, so the multigraded Hilbert series is literally a GL_m character. Passing to the stable GL_∞ setting makes the Schur expansion independent of the number of rows, and finite-m Hilbert series are recovered by setting all but m GL variables equal to zero.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is the direct conceptual mechanism behind universal Schur coefficients c_λ(n): identify the multigraded Hilbert series with a stable GL character, then c_λ(n) is a GL-Schur multiplicity, possibly after forgetting or summing the S_n side.

proof_techniques.name: Plethystic hook extraction via q−εu
proof_techniques.description: The identity f[q−εu]/(q+u) = Σ_{a,b} ⟨f,s_(a|b)⟩ q^a u^b turns the problem of finding all hook Schur coefficients of a symmetric function f into the problem of computing a two-variable plethystic specialization. It works because Schur functions vanish on the virtual alphabet q−εu unless they are hooks, and hooks evaluate to the monomial q^a u^b up to the common factor q+u.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the Hilbert series in the original problem, applying this specialization to the stable GL character would package all hook coefficients c_(a|b)(n) into one bivariate generating function. A combinatorial interpretation of that specialization would immediately give a hook interpretation.

proof_techniques.name: Use q-binomial/product expansions to interpret hook multiplicities
proof_techniques.description: In Proposition 1, a sum of one-variable hook coefficients q^{binomial(b+1,2)} [n−1 choose b]_q u^a is converted by the q-binomial theorem into the product Π_{i=1}^{n−1}(q^i+u). After dividing by q+u, the hook coefficients of A_n are read off from Π_{i=2}^{n−1}(q^i+u), giving a subset-sum interpretation.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the hook-coefficient generating function for the full Hilbert series can be reduced to a similar product or q-binomial sum, then the coefficients can be interpreted as subsets with prescribed size and weight, or as partitions inside a rectangle via Gaussian binomial coefficients.

proof_techniques.name: Reduce hook data to the alternating component by elementary skewing
proof_techniques.description: A major structural principle proposed in the paper is that S_n-hook isotypic components of E_n are obtained from the alternating component A_n by applying e_k^⊥ on the GL side: for μ = (k+1,1^{n−k−1}), the conjectured identity is ⟨E_n,s_μ⟩ = e_k^⊥ A_n. The operator e_k^⊥ has a concrete Schur-combinatorial meaning via the dual Pieri rule: it removes k cells with no two in the same row.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Although conjectural in the paper, this suggests a strategy for hook Schur coefficients: first understand the alternating component, then apply vertical-strip removal rules to derive other hook-related coefficients.

proof_techniques.name: Exploit small-row specializations to constrain stable Schur coefficients
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses the fact that evaluating the stable GL character in one or two GL variables retains only GL partitions of length at most one or two. The one-variable specialization is governed by the classical coinvariant/Macdonald formula, and the two-variable specialization is governed by ∇(e_n). Length conjectures then allow partial reconstruction of stable coefficients from these low-row specializations.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A hook partition λ has known length, so evaluating the Hilbert series at m = ℓ(λ) or nearby values may isolate enough information to recover c_λ(n), especially if independent length bounds or triangularity are available.

proof_techniques.name: Length filtration on the GL side
proof_techniques.description: The decomposition E_n = Σ_d E_n^{(d)} separates terms according to the number of parts of the GL partition λ. This filtration interacts naturally with finite-row restriction, because s_λ vanishes in fewer variables than ℓ(λ), and with elementary skewing, because e_k^⊥ removes vertical strips and changes possible lengths.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For hook λ = (a+1,1^b), the length is b+1. A proof could try to isolate the length-b+1 part of the Hilbert series and then use triangularity or specialization in exactly b+1 variables to read off c_λ(n).

proof_techniques.name: Bridge to Delta operators and LLT combinatorics
proof_techniques.description: The Delta-by-Skewing conjecture identifies certain elementary skewings of E_n with ∆'_{e_a}e_n, a Macdonald-eigenoperator expression. The appendix recalls the Delta-conjecture formula expressing ∆'_{e_k}e_n using vertical-strip LLT polynomials and path statistics; at t = 1 this becomes a sum involving skew Schur functions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a hook coefficient can be expressed through one of these skewed E_n components, existing or conjectural Delta-conjecture combinatorics may supply the desired counting objects and statistics.

proof_techniques.name: Evaluate GL Schur functions at k ones to obtain dimension polynomials
proof_techniques.description: The hook-content formula s_λ(k) = Π_{cells}(k+j−i)/h(i,j) turns the stable Schur expansion into polynomial formulas in the number k of variable sets. The paper gives examples of total and alternating dimensions as positive sums of binomial-coefficient polynomials.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Knowing the Hilbert series or dimensions for enough values of m can, in principle, be inverted against the known polynomials s_λ(m). This may help recover or check proposed formulas for hook c_λ(n).

other_useful_info: Important conjectural statements in the paper that may guide a proof but are not established there: Conjecture 1, Delta-by-Skewing, states in displayed form that for n = a+b+1, ∆'_{e_a} e_n(z) = ((e_b^⊥ ⊗ Id) E_n)(q,t;z). Conjecture 2, Hook-Components, states that for the S_n-hook μ = (k+1,1^{n−k−1}), one has ⟨E_n,s_μ⟩ = e_k^⊥ A_n, where A_n = ⟨E_n,e_n(z)⟩ is the alternating component. Conjecture 3, Coefficient-Length, states that ℓ(⟨E_n,s_μ⟩) = n−μ_1 for every μ ⊢ n. Conjecture 4 gives a hook-hook multiplicity formula: for a+b+1 = n and c_(a|b) = ⟨E_n,s_(a|b)(z)⟩, c_(a|b)[q−εu]/(q+u) = [n−1 choose b]_q (q^2+u)(q^3+u)···(q^b+u); this directly encodes the multiplicities of GL-hook Schur functions inside S_n-hook isotypic components. The paper states that Wallace [31] recently showed Conjecture 1 implies Conjecture 4. The paper also supplies explicit Schur-⊗-Schur expansions of E_n for n ≤ 6 and length components of A_7, which can be used as test data. Section 6 discusses an experimental e-positivity phenomenon for F_n = E_n[1+q;z], with elementary-basis coefficients d_ν conjecturally Schur positive; it proves the identity d_(n) = A_n using the hook-specialization calculation. The appendix recalls the Delta-conjecture formula for ∆'_{e_k}e_n in terms of vertical-strip LLT polynomials, which is a possible source of combinatorial models if the Delta-by-Skewing bridge is available.
why_relevant: Studies diagonal harmonics as simultaneous GL_k and S_n modules and formulates structural decompositions relevant to universal Schur coefficients. It is especially useful for understanding how GL_k characters encode multigraded Hilbert series.
